title: The CropSync Harvest Crisis: Database Full
summary: Harvest season hits and the farm data platform freezes. New crop readings won't save, but nobody changed the code.

resolution.root_cause: The RDS MySQL instance cropsync-prod-db (db.t3.medium, 20GB allocated storage) ran out of disk space. A runaway logging query and accumulated binary logs consumed all available storage, causing all INSERT and UPDATE operations to fail with 'The table is full' errors.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the RDS instance has zero free storage space
Player proposes increasing the allocated storage or enabling storage auto-scaling on the RDS instance
Player identifies that binary logs or accumulated data caused the storage to fill up

learning_objectives: RDS instances have a fixed storage allocation that can be exhausted by data growth, logs, or temporary files
CloudWatch FreeStorageSpace metric is critical for monitoring RDS health and should always have an alarm
RDS storage auto-scaling can automatically increase storage when free space drops below a threshold
sop_steps: Check the database status. Run describe-db-instances (a command that shows details about your database) and look at DBInstanceStatus. If it says 'storage-full', the database has run out of disk space and cannot write any new data.

Verify by checking the FreeStorageSpace metric in CloudWatch (AWS's monitoring service). This metric shows how many bytes of disk space remain. If it is at zero, the database is completely out of room.

Figure out what is using all the space. Check three things: (1) Query information_schema.tables to see how large each database table has grown. (2) Check binary log retention -- binary logs are files the database writes to record every change, used for backups and replication, but they take up disk space. (3) Review the slow query log and general log settings in the parameter group (a collection of database configuration settings). Verbose logging can quietly consume gigabytes.

Give the database more disk space immediately. Use the modify-db-instance command with --allocated-storage set to a larger value (e.g., 50GB) and --apply-immediately. For most database types, this does not cause any downtime.

Clean up the binary logs that have been piling up. Call mysql.rds_set_configuration to reduce how many hours of binary logs the database keeps. Shorter retention means old logs are deleted sooner, freeing up space.

Turn off verbose logging that was left on. In the parameter group, set slow_query_log and general_log back to reasonable levels. These logs are useful for debugging but should not stay at high verbosity in production.

Turn on storage auto-scaling so this cannot happen again. Use modify-db-instance with --max-allocated-storage set to a safe ceiling (e.g., 100GB). With auto-scaling enabled, RDS automatically adds more disk space when free space drops below 10% of the total.

Set up an early warning system. Create a CloudWatch alarm on FreeStorageSpace that alerts your team (via SNS, a notification service) when space drops below 4GB (warning) and 2GB (critical). This gives you time to act before the database fills up completely.
Add a monthly check of database storage trends to your operations routine. Reviewing how fast storage is growing lets you plan ahead instead of reacting to emergencies.

related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of your actual data filling the disk, a temporary file used by the database engine (called ibtmp1) grows out of control. This happens when the database runs long or complex queries that need temporary scratch space. The error message mentions /rdsdbdata/tmp/ paths instead of your own tables. Restarting the database reclaims the space, but the real fix is optimizing those queries or limiting how much temporary space the database can use.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Monitor FreeStorageSpace alongside how long queries take to run. In the parameter group, set bounded values for temptable_max_ram and temptable_max_mmap -- settings that cap how much temporary space the database can use.

related_failure_modes.scenario: RDS instance reaches maximum allocated storage limit
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Storage auto-scaling is turned on, but the maximum limit was set too low. Auto-scaling stops adding space once it hits that ceiling, even if data keeps growing. The tricky part is that alerts might not fire because auto-scaling appeared to be handling the situation -- until it could not anymore.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set the maximum storage limit to at least 26 percent above your current usage. Review this ceiling every few months as your data grows to make sure it still has enough headroom.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Binary log replication lag filling storage on a read replica
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The primary database has plenty of space, but its read replica (a copy of the database used for handling read traffic) falls behind on processing changes. The unprocessed change logs (called relay logs) pile up on the replica and fill its disk. Write failures only show up on the replica, not the primary, which makes it confusing to diagnose.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Monitor FreeStorageSpace on every replica individually, not just the primary. Keep binary log retention as short as acceptable. Set up an alert on ReplicaLag -- a metric that shows how far behind the replica is.

sop_practices: Always turn on storage auto-scaling when you create a production database. Set the maximum storage limit high enough to cover at least 6 months of expected data growth. Auto-scaling automatically adds disk space when free space drops below 10%, so you never run out unexpectedly.

Set up a CloudWatch alarm on FreeStorageSpace for every database the moment you create it -- do not wait until after the first storage emergency. Early warnings give you time to act before writes start failing.

Treat binary log retention and slow query log settings as part of your infrastructure setup, not as temporary debugging switches. Review these settings in your parameter group (the database's configuration template) to make sure they are not quietly consuming disk space.
Include database storage trends in your monthly capacity reviews. Checking how fast storage is growing each month lets you spot problems early and plan upgrades before they become emergencies.

glossary.RDS: Amazon Relational Database Service. A managed service that runs database engines like MySQL, PostgreSQL, and others so you do not have to install or maintain the software yourself.
glossary.allocated storage: The total disk space assigned to an RDS instance when you create or modify it. Once set, it can be increased but never decreased.
glossary.FreeStorageSpace: A CloudWatch metric that reports how many bytes of disk space remain on an RDS instance. When it reaches zero, the database can no longer write data.
glossary.storage auto-scaling: An RDS feature that automatically increases allocated storage when free space drops below 10 percent of the total. You set a maximum limit to control costs.
glossary.binary logs: Files that MySQL writes to record every change made to the database. Used for replication and point-in-time recovery, but they consume disk space until purged.
glossary.gp3: A general-purpose SSD storage type backed by Amazon EBS. Offers baseline performance of 3,000 IOPS and 125 MiB/s throughput regardless of volume size.
glossary.CloudWatch alarm: A rule that watches a metric and changes state (OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA) when the metric crosses a threshold you define. Can trigger notifications or automated actions.
glossary.Multi-AZ: A deployment option that maintains a standby replica of your RDS instance in a different Availability Zone for automatic failover during outages.

components.name: ALB (cropsync-prod-lb)
components.role: Receives traffic from farmers and IoT sensors and distributes it to the API instances
components.failure_impact: If the ALB fails, no traffic reaches the API and all farmer-facing operations stop

components.name: EC2 instances (cropsync-api-01, cropsync-api-02, t3.medium)
components.role: Run the CropSync API that handles sensor data ingestion and irrigation schedule management
components.failure_impact: If instances fail, API requests return errors and farmers cannot log irrigation schedules

components.name: RDS MySQL 8.0 (cropsync-prod-db, db.t3.medium, 20GB gp3)
components.role: Stores sensor readings and irrigation schedules as the primary data store
components.failure_impact: If storage is exhausted, all INSERT and UPDATE operations fail and the platform becomes read-only

components.name: CloudWatch
components.role: Monitors RDS metrics including FreeStorageSpace, DatabaseConnections, and WriteIOPS
components.failure_impact: If monitoring is absent, storage exhaustion goes undetected until write failures begin

system.data_flow: IoT sensors and farmer browsers send requests through the ALB to the EC2 API servers, which read from and write to the RDS MySQL instance. CloudWatch collects RDS metrics every 60 seconds.
system.what_broke: The RDS instance exhausted its 20GB of allocated storage. Data growth (14.8GB), binary logs (3.2GB), and slow query logs (1.8GB) filled the disk. MySQL cannot write when storage is zero, so all INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations fail with error 1114.

progressive_clues: application is timing out on database writes -- what could cause a database to reject write operations?
check the RDS instance metrics in CloudWatch -- storage-related metrics reveal database health
FreeStorageSpace metric is at zero -- RDS instance has run out of disk space, causing all write operations to fail
increase the allocated storage for the RDS instance, and enable storage auto-scaling to prevent recurrence
